metadata.title: Claude, Scouting
metadata.subtitle: The Research Plugin
metadata.audience: Practitioners and makers following the channel's Claude workflows
metadata.one_idea: Market research is the thing every operator knows they should do and never has time for; Claude can scout your market, competitors, and customers — but treat outputs as leads to verify, not settled fact.

props.command: How do I actually research my market without burning a whole week on it?

beats.narration_text: Namaste — this is Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. Market research is the thing every operator knows they should do and never finds time for. Point Claude at your market, your competitors, your customers — and it scouts in an hour what used to eat a day. Here's how far that goes, and where you still have to check its work.

props.command: How do I actually research my market without burning a whole week on it?

beats.narration_text: Start with the work you already avoid.

props.title: The Unpaid Homework
props.sub: Start with the work you already avoid.

beats.narration_text: You might not call yourself a researcher. But if you've ever spent an afternoon reading articles to understand a market before entering it, or pulled together everything on a competitor before a pitch — that's the work. You've done it. It just never made it onto the calendar.
beats.show: A single card flips from 'Not my job' to 'You already do this' — the unclaimed task.

props.question: A single card flips from 'Not my job' to 'You already do this' — the unclaimed task.

beats.narration_text: Here's what it usually looks like. An afternoon vanishing into open tabs and printouts, trying to hold a stack of sources in your head at once. Useful work — but slow, and easy to keep postponing.
beats.show: A still of a desk buried in articles and reports, one person mid-read; slow push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a person at a desk swamped in papers and open articles, documentary duotone. No real brands or logos.

beats.narration_text: Point research support at the same pile and the shape changes. Give it a topic and a set of sources, and you get in about an hour what used to take a day of reading and note-taking. The bottleneck was never your intelligence — it was your time.
beats.show: A tall 'a day' bar collapses down to a short 'an hour' bar as the words land.

beats.narration_text: And what comes back isn't a summary.

props.title: Synthesis, Not Summary
props.sub: And what comes back isn't a summary.

beats.narration_text: Give Claude a topic and a stack of sources — articles, reports, documents — and it pulls out the common themes, the places they conflict, and the takeaways that matter. Your sources flow in; structured understanding comes out.
beats.show: Sources stream in on the left; themes, conflicts, and takeaways resolve on the right — SourceFlow.

beats.narration_text: The difference that matters: it doesn't tell you what each source says, one by one. It tells you what they mean together. A pile of separate accounts becomes one picture — the throughline no single article stated on its own.
beats.show: Scattered source-dots drift together and fuse into one bright node.

beats.narration_text: That's the line between summary and synthesis. A summary just shrinks each source. Synthesis reads across them — and it's the version you can actually make a decision on.
beats.show: Two stacked panels: 'Summary: each source, smaller' above 'Synthesis: the pattern across'.

props.label: Summary versus synthesis.
props.caption: That's the line between summary and synthesis. A summary just shrinks each source. Synthesis reads across them — and it'

beats.narration_text: Now aim it outward — at the market.

props.title: Scouting the Field
props.sub: Now aim it outward — at the market.

beats.narration_text: Pointed at your market, the same engine does four jobs. Competitor intelligence. A structured market review. Gap analysis — what's covered and what isn't. And citation tracking, so every claim keeps its source.
beats.show: Four chips resolve onto the stage: Competitor intel · Market review · Gap analysis · Citations.

beats.narration_text: Competitor intelligence first. Claude reads how rivals position themselves, what they're building, how they talk to customers — and where the gaps sit. It works from public information, and gets sharper if you've saved their materials to your folders.
beats.show: A still of a lone figure surveying a skyline of storefronts and signage — scouting the field. Slow push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a figure looking out over a landscape of generic storefronts and signage, documentary duotone. No real logos or brands.

beats.narration_text: What comes back is a map, not a list. Where each competitor sits on price and focus, who's crowded together, and the corner nobody's standing in. That empty quadrant is often the whole point of the exercise.
beats.show: Dots drop onto a two-axis positioning map; clusters form; one empty quadrant pulses terracotta.

beats.narration_text: Every finding stays attached to where it came from. Ask for proper attribution and Claude tracks the sources and formats the citations — and it'll hook into a reference manager if you already run one.
beats.show: A finding card with a source-tag threading back to a labeled citation stack.

props.label: Every claim, sourced.
props.caption: Every finding stays attached to where it came from. Ask for proper attribution and Claude tracks the sources and formats

beats.narration_text: Gap analysis runs the same move on your own coverage. After reading across a topic, Claude shows what's been handled well and what's thin — in the market, or in your own research. You see the hole before it costs you.
beats.show: A grid of coverage cells fills in; one region stays empty and gets ringed.

beats.narration_text: Most of this rides on how you ask. For a competitor deep dive, you'd type something like this: everything public on their positioning, pricing model, audience, recent launches, and customer sentiment — then the strengths and weaknesses. Specific in, specific out.
beats.show: Onda code-block: a composer prompt for a competitive deep dive types in.

props.code:
Compile everything public on Rival Co:
positioning, pricing model, target audience,
recent launches, customer sentiment —
then their strengths and weaknesses.

props.title: Cowork
props.code:
Compile everything public on Rival Co:
positioning, pricing model, target audience,
recent launches, customer sentiment —
then their strengths and weaknesses.

beats.narration_text: Or point it at a batch you've already collected. Drop a folder of articles on a topic into Cowork and ask for the trends they agree on, the places they disagree, and what it means for you specifically. Synthesis on your own shelf.
beats.show: A still of a folder of saved clippings and printouts on a desk, ready to be read; gentle push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a labeled folder spilling saved articles and printouts on a desk, documentary duotone. No real publications.

beats.narration_text: Research earns its keep at the decision.

props.title: Research That Decides
props.sub: Research earns its keep at the decision.

beats.narration_text: Say you're weighing a new market. Ask Claude to research it — how big, who's already there, what customers actually need, where the openings are — and it returns a structured briefing: context, players, pain points, opportunity gaps, and the things to watch.
beats.show: A briefing document assembles section by section: Context · Players · Pain points · Gaps · Watch-outs.

props.label: One structured briefing.
props.caption: Say you're weighing a new market. Ask Claude to research it — how big, who's already there, what customers actually need

beats.narration_text: But the output is only as sharp as the purpose you name. 'Research AI trends' gives you a bland overview. 'Research AI trends so I can decide whether to add AI services to my practice' gives you focused, usable intelligence. The purpose shapes the answer.
beats.show: One vague arrow sprays wide into fog; the purpose-tagged arrow narrows to a single bright target.

beats.narration_text: This is the moment research is really for — standing at the edge of a decision you can't take back, about to commit time or money to a direction. Better to have scouted the ground first than to find out after you've stepped.
beats.show: A still of a lone figure at the edge of a ridge, looking out over unfamiliar terrain at dawn. Slow push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a lone figure at a ridge edge overlooking new terrain at dawn, documentary duotone. No real places.

beats.narration_text: For a straight choice — say, a free-trial model versus a freemium one — ask for the advantages and drawbacks of each for your kind of product, with real examples. Claude lays them side by side on the evidence, so you're deciding on a comparison instead of a gut feeling.
beats.show: Two option columns fill with weighted pros and cons; a balance beam settles between them.

beats.narration_text: One rule holds all of this together.

props.title: Leads, Not Verdicts
props.sub: One rule holds all of this together.

beats.narration_text: Here's the discipline. Treat what comes back as leads, not verdicts. It works from public and available information, which can be wrong or out of date. So: verify the load-bearing claims, check the sources, and keep the final judgment yours.
beats.show: Three caution chips resolve: Verify the claims · Check the sources · Judgment stays yours.

beats.narration_text: And when sources disagree, don't smooth it over. Ask Claude to flag the contradictions and leave them standing. Experts disagreeing isn't a mess to clean up — it's a map of where the real uncertainty lives, and that's information you want.
beats.show: Conflicting data points light up and get ringed rather than averaged; the disagreements glow terracotta.

beats.narration_text: Which leaves one habit worth building. Picture the operator with their head down in delivery — shipping the work, never once looking up at the market moving around them.
beats.show: A still of a person hunched over their work at a cluttered desk, blinds shut to the world outside. Slow push toward them.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a person hunched over work in a closed-in space, blinds shut, documentary duotone. Continuity with B21 (vox_run R1).

beats.narration_text: Now the one who spends an hour a month scouting — who knows their market cold. Same desk, window open. Same effort, better decisions. That's the whole return on the habit. Set the time aside, and let Claude do the scouting.
beats.show: Continuous move from the closed desk to the same desk with the window open, the person looking up, a market map on the wall. Same room grammar as B20.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: same person and desk, window now open, looking up, a market map visible; same duotone. Continuity with B20 (vox_run R1).

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Market research is the work everyone means to do and never schedules. Point Claude at it and you get synthesis, not summary — what your sources mean together. It scouts competitors, maps gaps, and turns a market into a structured briefing you can decide on. Name your purpose and the output sharpens. And treat every finding as a lead to verify — the scouting is Claude's, the judgment is yours.

props.command: I'm considering entering [market or opportunity]. Act as my market scout: (1) size it and name who's already there, (2) map the main competitors on positioning and where the gaps are, (3) list the top three things customers in this space actually need, and (4) flag every claim you're unsure about so I know what to verify before I commit.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude: I'm considering entering a market — act as my scout. Size it, name who's already there, map the competitors and the gaps, tell me the three things customers actually need, and flag every claim you're unsure about so I know what to verify. It turns this chapter into a scouting run on your real market — and notice it marks its own soft spots, which is exactly where your judgment comes in.

props.command: I'm considering entering [market or opportunity]. Act as my market scout: (1) size it and name who's already there, (2) map the main competitors on positioning and where the gaps are, (3) list the top three things customers in this space actually need, and (4) flag every claim you're unsure about so I know what to verify before I commit.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Scouting. Kore, in for Humanitarians AI.
